title: Stacking Encoder Blocks
concept: Stacking multiple encoder blocks builds increasingly abstract representations of input text. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: The Decoder Block.'

exercises.prompt: What does a single encoder block produce as its output?

options.text: Representations of the text that capture word meanings in context
A translated version of the text in a different language
An image representing the words in the sentence

exercises.explain: An encoder block processes text input and outputs numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the meaning and context of the words.

cards.title: Going Deeper
cards.body: A single encoder block can spot basic relationships between words. But what if you want to understand sarcasm, complex logic, or the deep meaning of a whole essay? That is where stacking comes in!

cards.title: What is Stacking?
cards.body: Stacking encoder blocks means feeding the output of one encoder block directly into the next. Each layer builds on the previous one's work, helping the AI grasp complex grammar, tone, and deep contextual meanings.

cards.title: The Skyscraper View
cards.body: Think of stacking encoder blocks like building a skyscraper. The ground floor (first block) only sees the raw materials and street-level details like spelling and basic words. As you stack floors higher, the view changes. By the top floor (final block), you can see the entire layout of the city—understanding the big picture and how everything fits together.

cards.title: Layer by Layer
cards.body: In a 6-layer encoder stack, Layer 1 might just notice that 'bank' is near the word 'river'. Layer 3 figures out that 'bank' means a muddy slope, not a financial building. Layer 6 understands the overall poetic mood of the sentence describing the river.

cards.title: Not Just Copy-Pasting
cards.body: You might think that adding more encoder blocks just repeats the exact same work. In reality, each block in the stack processes information at a different level of abstraction. The higher the block, the more sophisticated its understanding.

cards.title: Low vs. High Blocks
cards.body: Compare how text is processed at different levels of the encoder stack.

left.text: Focus on raw words, basic spelling, and immediate neighbors.

right.text: Focus on overall themes, sarcasm, and complex relationships.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the output of a lower encoder block in a stack?

options.text: It becomes the input for the next block above it.
It is immediately thrown away to save space.
It goes straight to the final output layer, skipping other blocks.
It is converted back into raw text characters.

exercises.explain: The output of one encoder block becomes the direct input for the next block above it in the stack.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Stacking encoder blocks helps the AI understand more complex patterns than a single block could.
exercises.explain: Stacking blocks allows the AI to learn increasingly complex, high-level features of the text.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of encoder stacks.
exercises.sentence: Stacking encoder blocks allows the model to build more [blank] representations of the input text.
exercises.explain: As representations pass up the stack, they lose raw surface detail and gain abstract, high-level meaning.

exercises.prompt: Model A stacks six encoder blocks to analyze a complex article. Model B uses only one single encoder block. Which model is likely to struggle with understanding abstract concepts in the text?

options.text: Model A, because it only looks at individual characters without context.
Model B, because it does not stack blocks to build abstract representations.
Both models are equally good at understanding deep, complex meaning.

exercises.explain: Model B is using the output of a single block to make predictions, which lacks the deep, abstract reasoning built by stacked layers.

exercises.prompt: Why do we stack multiple encoder blocks on top of each other instead of just using one?

options.text: It forces the model to process only one word at a time, forgetting the rest.
It allows the model to build deeper, more abstract representations of the text.
It reduces the model's vocabulary size to save memory.
It converts the text into a completely different language instantly.

exercises.explain: By stacking encoder blocks, the network builds a deep hierarchy of understanding—going from simple words to abstract concepts. Next up: The Decoder Block.
